image.generation_prompt:
The North Atlantic, 400 miles south of Newfoundland, 11:40 PM, April 14th, 1912. The RMS Titanic lists almost imperceptibly to starboard, her hull groaning beneath the waterline where steel has peeled back against ice at twenty-two and a half knots. Steam vents screech from the funnels into a sky of absolute, merciless clarity — no moon, stars burning cold and indifferent above a glassy black sea that is, tonight, catastrophically calm.

Three men occupy three separate worlds within the same dying vessel. In the Marconi room on the Boat Deck, Senior Wireless Operator Jack Phillips hunches over his transmitter, his face bathed in the pale amber glow of the instrument panel, fingers moving with mechanical precision across the key. His uniform jacket hangs unbuttoned, collar loosened, dark crescents beneath his eyes betraying fourteen continuous hours at the set. He is not afraid yet — he is working, which is the same as faith. Beside him, blue sparks leap from the transmission arc with each pulse of CQD, stuttering light against the brass fittings.

Four decks below, in the forward boiler room, Second Engineer Herbert Morrison stands waist-deep in the nightmare geometry of the lower ship — pipes sweating, pressure gauges twitching, bilge water already cold and black around his boots. His face is streaked with coal dust and purpose, lit from below by the hellish orange of the furnace doors, shadows thrown upward across the riveted iron walls. He moves with the deliberate calm of a man who understands exactly what is happening and refuses to honor it with panic.

On D Deck, First Class Steward Charles Whitmore moves through a corridor of impossible elegance — mahogany paneling, plush carpet, electric sconces burning warm gold, the entire fantasy of the ship still intact and glowing around him — knocking on stateroom doors with measured authority, his white gloves immaculate, his voice steady, coaxing passengers into lifebelts with the professional grace of a man serving tea. His eyes betray everything his voice conceals: a precision of terror sublimated entirely into duty.

The image should carry the weight of beauty about to be extinguished — gorgeous, doomed, suffused with the amber warmth of electric light against cold darkness pressing in from every direction.

title: Titanic: The Final Hours
premise: April 14, 1912, 11:40 PM. RMS Titanic has struck an iceberg 400 miles south of Newfoundland. The ship's designer knows she will sink in under two hours. There are lifeboats for half the passengers. Three people — a senior wireless operator, a second engineer, and a first class steward — have ninety minutes to make decisions that will determine who survives, and what the record will show about how it ended.

pressureEvents: 11:55 PM — Boiler Room No. 6 floods completely; the List increases to 5 degrees to starboard
12:45 AM — First lifeboat (No. 7) lowered with only 28 of 65 seats filled; officers begin enforcing women-and-children protocol at gunpoint
01:20 AM — Water reaches the Forecastle Deck; the Marconi Room power fluctuates as Engine Room No. 1 begins to flood
01:50 AM — The last davit-ready lifeboats (Nos. 2 and 4) are lowered; only Collapsibles A, B, C, and D remain
02:10 AM — The bow submerges entirely; the stern rises clear of the water; structural failure is imminent

open_threads.historical_record: The precise moment at which Captain Smith and Thomas Andrews determined the ship was doomed and the timeline communicated to officers remains disputed. Andrews reportedly told Smith the ship could stay afloat 'an hour, an hour and a half, possibly longer' shortly after the collision; the exact figure and timing of this communication were never definitively established in either inquiry. The flooding rate of Boiler Room No. 5 and the sequence of compartment flooding were reconstructed from survivor testimony and remain subject to ongoing revision by naval architects and marine forensic investigators, most recently in the 1996 and 2005 expeditions to the wreck site.

The precise time of the first distress transmission from Titanic is documented as 00:15 AM ship's time (CQD sent by Phillips), with Carpathia's operator Harold Cottam receiving the call at 00:25 AM Carpathia time. The order from Chief Engineer Bell to maintain steam was not directly testified to in either inquiry; its substance is inferred from the fact that the engineering staff maintained power and steam until the final minutes. No engineering officer survived to testify. The instructions given by Purser Herbert McElroy to stewards during mustering were not recorded in either inquiry transcript.

Lifeboat No. 7 was the first lifeboat lowered from RMS Titanic, departing at approximately 00:40 AM from the starboard side under the supervision of First Officer William Murdoch. The British Board of Trade Inquiry recorded 28 persons in Boat No. 7 against a capacity of 65. The low occupancy of the first boats was attributed in both inquiries to passenger reluctance to leave the ship in the early stages of the evacuation, combined with officers' uncertainty about the safe load for the davits. Fifth Officer Harold Lowe testified that he believed the Welin davits could hold a fully loaded boat but that this was not established practice at the time.

Charles Lightoller testified at both inquiries that he enforced a strict women-and-children-only policy on the port side and that he did so on his own interpretation of the order, not on a direct command from Captain Smith specifying exclusion of men. First Officer Murdoch on the starboard side allowed men to board when no women were present or waiting. The discrepancy between port and starboard loading policies was examined at both inquiries and was never resolved into a single authoritative account of what Captain Smith actually ordered. Lightoller's port-side boats departed with lower average occupancy than Murdoch's starboard-side boats.

Leading Stoker Frederick Barrett survived and testified that Boiler Room No. 6 flooded rapidly after the collision and that the watertight door between Boiler Rooms No. 5 and No. 6 was closed. Barrett escaped through an emergency ladder. The sequence of flooding in the boiler rooms and the decisions made by engineering personnel regarding watertight doors are known only from Barrett's testimony and that of a small number of other surviving stokers and firemen. No engineering officer survived. The British Inquiry found that the watertight doors were closed as designed but noted that the flooding of Boiler Room No. 5 over the top of the watertight bulkhead was a critical factor in the ship's loss.

Harold Bride testified that the German steamship Frankfurt responded to Titanic's distress call but that her operator appeared not to understand the situation and asked 'What is the matter with you?' Phillips reportedly responded with an expletive and ceased communicating with Frankfurt to focus on Carpathia. The exchange with Frankfurt is documented in Bride's Senate testimony. Frankfurt's position was actually significantly further away than Carpathia (over 120 miles to the southwest), meaning she could not have arrived in time to assist. The British Inquiry ultimately focused on the exchange as a structural example of the critical need for unified, standardized wireless protocols across different commercial operators.

The documented departure numbers for each lifeboat were recorded by officers and crew and formed the basis for the occupancy figures examined at both inquiries. The British Board of Trade Inquiry published a table of lifeboat departures, times, and recorded occupancies. These figures have been disputed by subsequent researchers; some boats' occupancy figures vary between the U.S. and British inquiry records. The total of 711 persons recorded as departing in lifeboats is the British Inquiry figure; the U.S. Inquiry produced slightly different totals. No single authoritative manifest of lifeboat occupancy exists that all subsequent historians have accepted without qualification.

Survivor testimony and the physical evidence of the wreck confirm that electrical power was maintained aboard Titanic until very close to the final sinking, with lights reported burning until the ship's final plunge or nearly so. Harold Bride testified that the wireless power fluctuated in the final hour and that the emergency dynamo was brought into use. The precise sequence of dynamo failures and the communications, if any, between the Marconi Room and the engine room regarding power prioritization are not documented in either inquiry. No engineering personnel who managed the dynamos survived.

Thomas Andrews's assessment of the flooding rate and the ship's survivability was communicated to Captain Smith shortly after the collision. The substance of this assessment — that the ship had approximately one to one and a half hours — is attested by multiple witnesses including Second Officer Lightoller and others who were present or were told of it. No notebook or written document by Andrews from the night of the sinking has been recovered or authenticated. Andrews's papers went down with the ship. The flooding rate calculation itself has been reconstructed by naval architects using the damage survey from the wreck, most comprehensively in the work of naval architect Edward Wilding, who testified at the British Inquiry, and in subsequent forensic analyses.

The treatment of Third Class passengers during the evacuation was examined at both inquiries and remains one of the most contested aspects of the disaster. The U.S. Senate Inquiry heard testimony from Third Class survivors including Olaus Abelseth that gates separating Third Class from upper decks were locked or guarded in the early stages of the evacuation. The British Board of Trade Inquiry examined this question and concluded that there was no deliberate policy of preventing Third Class passengers from reaching the boat deck, attributing the disparity in survival rates to language barriers, unfamiliarity with the ship's layout, and the location of Third Class accommodations at the bow and stern. Third Class survival rate was approximately 25 percent, compared to approximately 60 percent for First Class and approximately 42 percent for Second Class. The question of whether gates were locked and on whose authority has never been definitively resolved.

choice_echoes.historical_record: Thomas Andrews informed Captain Smith of the flooding assessment approximately 20 to 25 minutes after the collision, following a physical inspection of the forward flooded spaces, estimating the ship had one to one and a half hours. The flooding of Boiler Room No. 5 was confirmed by Leading Stoker Frederick Barrett, who was present when water entered. The ship carried 2,224 persons according to the British Board of Trade Inquiry figure (the U.S. Inquiry used a figure of 2,223). Third Class accommodations were located at the bow and stern of the ship, separated from upper decks by gates that were part of the immigration regulations requiring separation of steerage passengers.

Jack Phillips sent the first CQD distress call at 00:15 AM Titanic ship's time on April 15, 1912. Carpathia's operator Harold Cottam received the call at 00:25 AM Carpathia time and immediately informed Captain Arthur Rostron, who turned the ship and ordered full speed toward Titanic's reported position. Carpathia was approximately 58 miles southeast of Titanic's reported position. Chief Engineer Bell and all engineering officers remained at their posts throughout the sinking; none survived to provide direct testimony about orders received.

Lifeboat No. 7 was lowered at approximately 00:40 AM with 28 persons aboard against a certified capacity of 65. It was supervised by First Officer William Murdoch on the starboard side. Passenger reluctance was cited by multiple officers as the primary reason for low initial occupancy. The Welin double-acting davits with which Titanic was equipped were certified to lower a fully loaded boat, but officers testified they were uncertain of this at the time and lowered boats partially filled with the intention of loading more passengers from the gangway doors, a plan that was never executed.

Charles Lightoller enforced women-and-children-only on the port side throughout the evacuation. He testified that he would not have allowed men into boats while women remained on the ship. His port-side boats departed with lower average occupancy than the starboard-side boats loaded by First Officer Murdoch, who allowed men to board when no women were present. Collapsible D, the last boat Lightoller loaded, departed at approximately 02:05 AM with approximately 44 persons; Lightoller held men back with a pistol to prevent rushing. The total number of persons who died while spaces remained in lifeboats cannot be precisely calculated from the historical record.

Boiler Room No. 6 flooded immediately after the collision. Leading Stoker Frederick Barrett testified that water burst through the ship's side at the forward end of Boiler Room No. 6 and that he escaped through the emergency ladder as the watertight door closed. Boiler Room No. 5 subsequently flooded over the top of the watertight bulkhead between compartments 5 and 6 as the bow sank lower. All engineering officers who remained in the engine and boiler rooms died. The watertight doors functioned as designed but could not prevent flooding over the tops of bulkheads that did not extend to the full height of the ship.

Harold Bride testified that Frankfurt responded to Titanic's distress call but that her operator did not appear to understand the situation. Phillips dismissed Frankfurt and concentrated on Carpathia. Frankfurt was too far away to assist, but her delayed comprehension highlighted the flaws of fractured wireless networks. This systemic breakdown illustrated the critical need for standardized wireless emergency protocols, which were subsequently incorporated into the 1914 SOLAS convention.

The British Board of Trade Inquiry published a complete table of lifeboat departures with recorded occupancy figures. The total recorded occupancy across all 20 lifeboats and collapsibles was 711 persons against a total certified capacity of 1,178. Every lifeboat departed below capacity. The boat with the lowest recorded occupancy relative to capacity was Lifeboat No. 1, which departed with 12 persons against a capacity of 40. The figures were compiled from officer testimony and the accounts of survivors in each boat.

Electrical power was maintained aboard Titanic until very close to the final sinking. Multiple survivors reported lights burning until the ship's final plunge or nearly so. Harold Bride testified that the wireless power fluctuated and that the emergency storage battery was used in the final period of transmission. The last confirmed wireless transmission from Titanic was received at approximately 02:17 AM. The engineering staff maintained the dynamos until the engine rooms flooded; all engineering officers died at their posts.

Thomas Andrews calculated that the flooding of five compartments — the forepeak, three forward holds, and Boiler Room No. 6 — was fatal and that the ship would sink. He communicated this to Captain Smith shortly after the collision. Naval architect Edward Wilding, testifying at the British Board of Trade Inquiry, calculated from the flooding evidence that approximately 12 square feet of hull area had been opened to the sea. Andrews's assessment that the ship had one to one and a half hours proved accurate; the ship sank approximately two hours and forty minutes after the collision. No written document by Andrews from the night of the sinking has been recovered.

Third Class passengers had a survival rate of approximately 25 percent (approximately 178 of 710 Third Class passengers survived, though exact figures vary between inquiries). First Class survival rate was approximately 60 percent. Multiple Third Class survivors testified to gates being locked or guarded. The British Board of Trade Inquiry concluded that no deliberate policy of exclusion existed but acknowledged that Third Class passengers faced structural and linguistic barriers to reaching the boat deck. The U.S. Senate Inquiry was more critical of the treatment of Third Class passengers. No officer or crew member was found to have given a specific order to hold Third Class passengers below decks, and no such order was documented.

character_fates.name: Harold Bride

primary_source.citation: U.S. Senate Inquiry, Testimony of Harold Bride, April 15–May 25, 1912, 62nd Congress, 2nd Session, S. Doc. 726; New York Times, April 19, 1912

character_fates.historical_record: Harold Bride remained at the wireless set transmitting distress calls until approximately 02:10 AM, when Captain Smith released him from duty. He was washed off the ship as it sank and clung to an overturned collapsible lifeboat (Collapsible B) alongside Second Officer Lightoller and others. He was rescued by RMS Carpathia with frostbitten feet. He subsequently testified before both the U.S. Senate Inquiry (April 1912) and the British Board of Trade Inquiry (May 1912). He gave a first-person account to The New York Times published April 19, 1912.

character_fates.name: Jack Phillips

primary_source.citation: U.S. Senate Inquiry, Testimony of Harold Bride, April 15–May 25, 1912, 62nd Congress, 2nd Session, S. Doc. 726

character_fates.historical_record: Jack Phillips continued transmitting distress signals until power failed at approximately 02:17 AM. He was last seen by Harold Bride attempting to reach Collapsible B. He did not survive. His body, if recovered, was never identified. He was 25 years old. Bride testified that Phillips was still at the key when Bride left the Marconi Room.

character_fates.name: Chief Engineer Joseph Bell

primary_source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, Report of the Court, 1912, Cmd. 6352; Eaton, John P. and Haas, Charles A., Titanic: Triumph and Tragedy, 1986

character_fates.historical_record: Chief Engineer Joseph Bell and all 35 engineering officers remained at their posts in the engine and boiler rooms throughout the sinking, maintaining power and steam. None survived. Bell's body was never recovered. His actions were cited in the British Board of Trade Inquiry as exemplary. He was 51 years old at the time of his death.

character_fates.name: Second Officer Charles Lightoller

primary_source.citation: U.S. Senate Inquiry, Testimony of Charles Lightoller, April 19–20, 1912, 62nd Congress, 2nd Session, S. Doc. 726; British Board of Trade Inquiry, Days 12–13, May 1912

character_fates.historical_record: Charles Lightoller was the most senior surviving officer of RMS Titanic. He loaded and oversaw the lowering of all port-side lifeboats, enforcing a strict women-and-children-only policy. He was washed off the ship as it sank, was briefly sucked against a ventilation grate, and eventually reached Collapsible B, which he commanded. He was rescued by Carpathia. He was the primary witness at both the U.S. Senate Inquiry and the British Board of Trade Inquiry, where his testimony was extensive and at times contradicted by other witnesses. He later served in World War I and World War II, and died in 1952.

character_fates.name: Thomas Andrews

primary_source.citation: U.S. Senate Inquiry, Testimony of John Stewart (First Class Steward), 1912; Shan Bullock, Thomas Andrews, Shipbuilder, 1912

character_fates.historical_record: Thomas Andrews was seen by Steward John Stewart in the First Class Smoking Room at approximately 01:40 AM staring at a painting, but was later reliably spotted by multiple crew members on the Boat Deck frantically throwing deck chairs into the water as flotation devices and urging passengers into the final lifeboats before the ship's final plunge. He did not survive. His body was never recovered. He was 39 years old. Steward John Stewart and other crew members testified to seeing him in the Smoking Room in his final minutes. Andrews had informed Captain Smith of the flooding rate and the ship's fate approximately 20 to 25 minutes after the collision, following a physical inspection of the forward flooded spaces.

character_fates.name: Margaret Brown

primary_source.citation: U.S. Senate Inquiry, Testimony of Margaret Brown, 1912, 62nd Congress, 2nd Session, S. Doc. 726

character_fates.historical_record: Margaret Brown was loaded into Lifeboat No. 6 at approximately 00:55 AM. Lifeboat No. 6 departed with approximately 28 persons aboard, against a capacity of 65. Brown repeatedly urged Quartermaster Robert Hichens, who commanded the boat, to return for survivors in the water; Hichens refused. Brown organized the women in the boat to row and maintain morale. She was rescued by Carpathia and helped organize relief efforts for survivors aboard that ship. She later testified before the U.S. Senate Inquiry. The popular characterization of her as 'the Unsinkable Molly Brown' was a later theatrical invention; she was known in her lifetime as Maggie Brown.

historical_frame: The sinking of RMS Titanic resulted in the International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), first adopted in 1914, which established requirements for sufficient lifeboat capacity for all persons aboard and mandatory 24-hour wireless watch on passenger vessels.

The U.S. Senate Inquiry, chaired by Senator William Alden Smith, opened April 19, 1912, four days after the disaster, and heard testimony from 86 witnesses over 18 days; the British Board of Trade Inquiry, presided over by Lord Mersey, ran from May 2 to July 3, 1912, and examined 97 witnesses across 36 days of hearings.

At the time of her sinking, RMS Titanic carried lifeboats exceeding the legal minimum required by the British Board of Trade, which based its lifeboat table on vessel tonnage rather than passenger capacity; a ship of Titanic's gross tonnage of 46,328 tons was required to carry boats for 962 persons, a regulation written in 1894 and not updated before 1912.

immediate_outcome.summary: RMS Titanic sank at 02:20 AM on April 15, 1912, approximately 2 hours and 40 minutes after striking the iceberg at 23:40 on April 14. Of the 2,224 persons aboard, 710 survived and 1,514 died. RMS Carpathia, having received the distress call and steamed approximately 58 miles at full speed, arrived at the position at approximately 04:10 AM and completed the rescue of all surviving lifeboats by approximately 08:30 AM.
key_facts: RMS Titanic struck an iceberg at 23:40 on April 14, 1912 (ship's time)
RMS Titanic sank at 02:20 AM on April 15, 1912
2,224 persons were aboard according to the British Board of Trade Inquiry figure
20 lifeboats were carried, with a total certified capacity of 1,178 persons
The lifeboats departed with an average occupancy substantially below capacity; the British Inquiry calculated a total of 711 persons in all boats combined
RMS Carpathia received the first CQD distress call at 01:05 AM Carpathia time (approximately 00:15 AM Titanic ship's time) and arrived at approximately 04:10 AM
Carpathia recovered 710 survivors and brought them to New York, arriving April 18, 1912
The water temperature at the site was approximately 28°F (minus 2°C); survival in the water without a lifeboat was measured in minutes

description: It is 11:40 PM on April 14, 1912, and the RMS Titanic has struck an iceberg at 41°43'N 49°56'W. Three crew members — a wireless operator, a second engineer, and a first class steward — must coordinate across the sinking ship to keep the lights burning, the signals transmitting, and the lifeboats filled before the Atlantic claims her. Every decision has a price.

sections.text: The year is 1912, and the North Atlantic in April is a killing ground that no amount of engineering hubris has ever tamed. The White Star Line's RMS Titanic — 882 feet long, 46,328 gross tons, the largest moving object ever built by human hands — departed Southampton on April 10th carrying 2,224 souls across three classes and a crew of 908. Her builders at Harland and Wolff in Belfast fitted her with sixteen watertight compartments and a double-bottom hull, and the press of two continents obligingly called her unsinkable, a word that no naval architect at the firm ever used. The wireless apparatus aboard — a Marconi Type 1 spark-gap transmitter capable of reaching 400 miles in daylight — represents the most advanced communication technology afloat, and it has been busy all day relaying ice warnings from the Mesaba, the Baltic, and the Amerika, warnings that reached the bridge and were noted and set aside.

At 11:40 PM on April 14th, lookout Frederick Fleet spots a black mass in the flat, starless water and rings the crow's nest bell three times. First Officer Murdoch orders hard-a-starboard and full astern, but 46,000 tons of steel requires more than 37 seconds to answer a helm. The iceberg scrapes the starboard hull for ten seconds across a distance of 300 feet, and the damage — a series of buckled seams and popped rivets rather than a single clean gash — opens the first five watertight compartments to the sea. Naval architect Thomas Andrews, who is aboard as a guarantee of the ship's perfection, does the arithmetic in his stateroom within the hour and tells Captain Smith the result quietly: the ship has between one and two hours. There are lifeboats for 1,178 people. There are 2,224 people aboard.

On the Boat Deck, the night air is dead calm and twenty-eight degrees Fahrenheit — cold enough that breath fogs white and the rigging has begun to frost. The sea is a black mirror, utterly without swell, which is why the iceberg was invisible until it was thirty seconds away. Below decks, the smell of salt water in places it has never been before is beginning to reach the corridors: a cold, mineral wrongness beneath the familiar coal smoke and machine oil. The eight-piece band has assembled near the First Class entrance and is playing ragtime, a sound that carries across the still water with an almost indecent cheerfulness, because the officers have decided that music will prevent panic, and panic is the thing they fear most.

You are one of three people on this ship who understands, with professional clarity, what the next two hours will require. The wireless operator in his crackling Marconi Room knows that the nearest answering ship is 58 miles away through pack ice and that every minute of signal strength is a minute of lives. The second engineer in the flooding heat of Boiler Room No. 4 knows that the moment the dynamos go dark, the electric winches on the davits stop and the lifeboats stop moving. The first class steward on C-Deck knows that the boats are being lowered half-empty by officers who are afraid of the math, and that the gates to the lower decks are still locked. The ship will sink at approximately 2:20 AM. It is now 11:55 PM. You are standing at the exact point where your choices begin to matter.

character_entries.second_engineer: Your boots are in three inches of water now — you checked two minutes ago and it was two and a half, and the rate-of-change arithmetic you have done four times produces the same answer every time, which is the answer you do not want. The dynamo gauge on the bulkhead reads nominal. The winch circuits on the boat deck are drawing current in the uneven, stop-start pattern that means boats are actually moving, which means the one thing you came down here to do is being done, and the forty men of the Black Gang working the flats below you do not know that Boiler Room No. 6 is already a closed system and No. 5 is thirty-one inches deep and climbing. Bell said *your discretion, son* at 1:05, which is a chief engineer's way of saying the ledger is settled and the remainder is yours to spend. The bus bar switches are six inches from your left hand. Ninety seconds, both hands, twenty additional minutes of light on the boat deck — you have run the sequence in your head until it is as clean and certain as a pressure calculation, and it is clean and certain, except for the water in No. 4, which has been behaving in a way that no calculation has yet explained. The stokers are watching your face. Your hand is on the switch.
character_entries.first_class_steward: The ring of keys is biting into your left palm because you have been gripping it for forty minutes without noticing, and the C-Deck corridor smells of dinner still — braised lamb, good carpet, the faint cedar of expensive luggage — underneath the other smell, the one that has no business being here, the cold mineral wrongness advancing from below like a tide that has forgotten its boundaries. You have spent eleven years making the machinery of this ship invisible, ensuring that the distance between a First Class stateroom and a Third Class berth registered as natural law rather than a locked gate and a key ring, and the machinery is still running: the band is playing two decks above you, Lightoller is recording forty-one souls in a boat that left with twenty-eight, and somewhere below E-Deck the people you have never been required to see are watching water move across their corridor floor. The steward's office is thirty feet behind you. You know the load tests run in Southampton four days ago. You know what Margaret Brown has been counting from the rail. You know what Lightoller does not know you know. Your lifebelt is still in your right hand, still not on your body, which is perhaps the most honest thing about you at this moment — the one piece of the evening's official narrative you have not yet agreed to perform. The corridor is empty. You take one breath of cedar and cold salt and lamb and the specific, countable, preventable arithmetic of the night, and you turn.
character_entries.senior_wireless_operator: Ozone and burnt transformer resin — that is what you breathe in this room, have breathed for sixteen hours, and the smell has stopped registering except as a marker that the equipment is still running, which is the only fact that matters. Your right hand rests on the key with the particular looseness of a hand that has been transmitting since before midnight and knows it may need to transmit for another hour, and the receiver against your left ear is delivering static with one clean frequency beneath it — Carpathia, still there, still coming, seventeen knots through black water she cannot see any better than you could see the ice. The signal log is open to your left, Carpathia's 12:25 confirmation in your own handwriting, a position and a bearing and an ETA that you have done the arithmetic on and found adequate, barely, if the power holds. The floor is wrong beneath your feet — two degrees, maybe two and a half now — and Phillips beside you transmits without looking up, his face a closed door, and what he knows and you know and the man in the First Class smoking room does not know is that there are twenty minutes of transmitting power that do not yet exist but could, if someone closes two bus bar switches in the engine room, if someone can reach the speaking tube, if there is still an engineer at the other end of it. The speaking tube cap is in your left hand. You did not decide to pick it up. The static hisses against your palm like something alive, and you hold it there, at the edge of your mouth, and do not yet speak.

winConditions: Carpathia's coordinates are confirmed and relayed before the Marconi Room loses power
Dynamos remain operational until the final lifeboat clears the davits
At least 12 of 16 standard lifeboats are launched at or above 50 percent capacity
All three player characters reach a lifeboat or Collapsible before 02:10 AM
failConditions: Marconi Room loses power before Carpathia confirms her course and ETA
Dynamos fail before Boat 4 is lowered, leaving the boat deck in darkness
Fewer than half the lifeboats are launched with meaningful loads
Time expires at 02:20 AM with characters still aboard

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912 (Cmd. 6352); US Senate Inquiry, 1912

facts.content: RMS Titanic foundered at 2:20 AM ship's time on 15 April 1912.

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Fourth Officer Joseph Boxhall

facts.content: Titanic struck the iceberg at 11:40 PM ship's time on 14 April 1912.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Harold Bride and Joseph Boxhall

facts.content: Titanic's distress position, as calculated by Fourth Officer Boxhall and transmitted by wireless, was 41°46'N, 50°14'W.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Captain Arthur Rostron of Carpathia

facts.content: RMS Carpathia received Titanic's CQD distress call at 12:25 AM on 15 April 1912 and arrived at the position at approximately 4:10 AM, approximately 58 miles from her position when she received the call.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Captain Arthur Rostron

facts.content: Carpathia increased speed from her service speed of approximately 14 knots to approximately 17 knots — her maximum — after receiving Titanic's distress signal.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Harold Bride

facts.content: Jack Phillips began transmitting the CQD distress call at approximately 12:15 AM on 15 April 1912, approximately 35 minutes after the collision.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Harold Bride

facts.content: Harold Bride suggested to Jack Phillips that they transmit SOS in addition to CQD, as SOS was the newer international distress signal; Titanic was among the first ships to transmit SOS in a genuine distress situation.

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912 (Cmd. 6352)

facts.content: RMS Titanic carried 2,224 persons on her maiden voyage, comprising approximately 1,317 passengers and 908 crew.

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912 (Cmd. 6352)

facts.content: Titanic carried 20 lifeboats with a total certified capacity of 1,178 persons: 14 standard wooden lifeboats each certified for 65 persons, 2 emergency cutters each certified for 40 persons, and 4 Engelhardt collapsible boats each certified for 47 persons.

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912 (Cmd. 6352)

facts.content: 710 persons survived the sinking of RMS Titanic; 1,514 persons perished.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912

facts.content: Lifeboat No. 7, the first boat lowered from the starboard side at approximately 12:40 AM, carried approximately 28 persons against its certified capacity of 65.

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Sir Cosmo Duff-Gordon

facts.content: Emergency cutter No. 1, certified for 40 persons, was lowered at approximately 1:10 AM carrying only 12 persons.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912; multiple survivor testimonies

facts.content: Titanic's engineers maintained electrical power to the ship's systems, including the wireless room, until shortly before the ship foundered at 2:20 AM; the lights remained on until the final plunge.

source.citation: Harland and Wolff shipbuilding records; Titanic's electrical installation specifications as cited in the British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912
source.access_note: Harland and Wolff records not publicly available; specifications cited in British Board of Trade Inquiry (Cmd. 6352)

facts.content: Titanic was equipped with four 400-kilowatt steam-driven dynamos located in the turbine room aft of the reciprocating engine room, providing ship's service power at 100 volts DC.

source.citation: Marconi International Marine Communication Company equipment specifications; US Senate Inquiry, 1912

facts.content: Titanic's Marconi wireless transmitter operated at 5 kilowatts and had a guaranteed working range of 250 miles, with an actual range under good conditions of up to 2,000 miles at night.

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912; survivor testimonies of engine room crew

facts.content: Chief Engineer Joseph Bell remained at his post in the engine room and perished with the ship; he was last seen alive by surviving crew members at his station maintaining power.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Harold Bride; New York Times interview, 19 April 1912

facts.content: Harold Bride survived the sinking; he remained in the Marconi room transmitting until power failed, was washed off the ship when it foundered, and was pulled onto Collapsible B with injured feet, later rescued by Carpathia.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Harold Bride

facts.content: Jack Phillips remained at the wireless key transmitting distress calls until power failed and did not survive; his body was never recovered.

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Charles Lightoller; US Senate Inquiry, 1912

facts.content: Second Officer Charles Lightoller enforced a strict women-and-children-only policy on the port side, refusing to allow men to board even when boats were not full; he survived by clinging to overturned Collapsible B.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; testimony of steward John Stewart; British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912

facts.content: Thomas Andrews was last seen alive by survivor John Stewart in the First Class Smoking Room at approximately 2:00 AM, standing alone with his arms folded, staring at a painting; he did not survive and his body was never recovered.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Quartermaster Robert Hichens and Margaret Brown

facts.content: Margaret Brown was placed in Lifeboat No. 6 at approximately 12:55 AM and, according to survivor accounts, took an oar and urged the boat's crew to return for survivors in the water after the ship sank.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Harold Bride

facts.content: The ice warning transmitted by SS Mesaba at 9:40 PM on 14 April 1912, which described heavy pack ice and large icebergs in the latitude and longitude range directly ahead of Titanic, was received by the Marconi room but was never delivered to the bridge.

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912; testimony of Leading Stoker Frederick Barrett

facts.content: Boiler Room No. 6 began flooding immediately after the collision at 11:40 PM; stoker Frederick Barrett testified that water burst through the ship's side at the forward end of the boiler room and the watertight door was closed by electric control from the bridge.

source.citation: US Senate Inquiry, 1912; British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912; testimony of multiple witnesses including steward Henry Etches

facts.content: Thomas Andrews informed Captain Smith shortly after midnight that the ship could remain afloat for approximately one to two hours based on his assessment of the flooding in the forward compartments.

source.citation: British Board of Trade Inquiry, 1912; Welin Davit and Engineering Company specifications entered into evidence

facts.content: Titanic was fitted with Welin double-acting davits, which were designed and tested to handle lifeboats loaded to full capacity; the davit system was capable of launching more boats than the ship actually carried.

categories.name: Marconi Room Signals

terms.meaning: The established Marconi distress signal, read as 'Seek You, Danger' — the standard call for assistance before SOS was adopted
The newer international distress signal adopted at the 1906 Berlin Convention, simpler to tap in Morse and harder to misread — rarely used in practice by 1912
A wireless telegram sent via the Marconi apparatus, typically personal messages from passengers to shore or other ships
Operator shorthand for a ship's current position, used when relaying coordinates to rescue vessels
Continuing to transmit despite deteriorating conditions — a point of professional pride for Marconi operators
Designated listening periods when all operators were required to monitor the distress frequency — not always observed on commercial ships
The transmitter technology used aboard Titanic, producing a broad, crackling signal recognizable across hundreds of miles

categories.context: Used by wireless operators and officers when sending or discussing distress communications and ship-to-ship traffic.

categories.name: Black Gang Trade Argot

terms.meaning: Collective name for all engine-room crew — engineers, stokers, coal-trimmers, and greasers — named for the coal dust and grease that permanently stained their skin
A worker whose job is to shovel coal from the bunkers to within reach of the firemen at the furnace doors
The fireman who feeds coal directly into the furnace and manages the fire's intensity to maintain steam pressure
The steel floor gratings of the boiler room — 'water on the plates' was the first sign of serious flooding
To rake burning coal out of the furnaces and douse it — the procedure for safely shutting down a boiler under emergency conditions
The sealed bulkhead door between compartments — once closed from the bridge, it could not be opened from inside without special authority
The steam-driven electrical generators that powered the ship's lights, wireless, and lifeboat winches — keeping them running was the engineers' final mission

categories.context: Used by the engineers, firemen, trimmers, and greasers working in the boiler rooms and engine spaces below the waterline.

categories.name: Deck and Steward Service Language

terms.meaning: The visible lean of the ship to one side as water floods unevenly into the hull — a List to starboard meant the starboard boats were harder to swing out
The crane-like iron arm from which lifeboats were suspended and lowered — Titanic used Welin davits, capable of handling two boats per station
Informal crew slang for Third Class steerage passengers, derived from 'steerage'
The formal assembly of passengers at their assigned lifeboat stations — Titanic had never conducted a full muster drill with passengers
The rope and pulley system used to lower a lifeboat from the davit to the water — a jammed fall could strand a loaded boat mid-air
A crew member traveling as staff rather than a paying passenger — many stewards held this status and had no assigned lifeboat seat
The informal protocol, not a formal maritime law in 1912, by which officers prioritized women and children for lifeboat seats — interpretation varied wildly by officer

categories.context: Used by deck officers, stewards, and pursers in the management of passengers and ship operations above the waterline.

categories.name: Bridge and Navigation Terms

terms.meaning: In 1912 tiller-order convention, this commanded the helm to turn the wheel to port, swinging the bow to port — the opposite of modern usage
The order to reverse all engines at maximum power — reducing forward momentum but also removing rudder effectiveness
The lookout platform high on the foremast — the two lookouts there had no binoculars on the night of April 14th, the glasses having been locked away since Southampton
A region of pack ice and bergs dense enough to require reduced speed or an altered course — multiple warnings of one ahead were received and disregarded on April 14th
Navigation by calculating position from a known point using speed, heading, and elapsed time — the method used to relay Titanic's distress coordinates to Carpathia

categories.context: Used by officers, quartermasters, and lookouts in the operation and command of the vessel.

partialSuccessExamples: The Price of Gallantry: Carpathia is guided in and boats are well-filled, but the Black Gang and wireless operators go down with the ship — maximum passenger survival, crew lost
The Operator's Bargain: Signals are maintained and Carpathia arrives, but two lifeboats launched half-empty due to officer interference — 400 additional deaths
The Engineer's Hour: Lights stay on and boats are filled, but no SOS reaches Carpathia in time — survivors rescued only at dawn by a passing vessel, with severe hypothermia casualties
